steps.prompt: Which is the main verb of the first clause?

hints.sciunt: 'Sciunt' is a finite verb but belongs to the causal clause
hints.faciunt: 'Faciunt' is a finite verb in the indirect question clause
hints.Pater: 'Pater' is a noun in the vocative — direct address

hints.to send: 'To send' is 'mittere' — 'dimitte' comes from 'dimittere' (dis- + mittere, to send away/forgive)
hints.to dismiss from office: 'Dimittere' can mean 'to dismiss' but the primary sense here is 'to forgive'

hints.present indicative: Present indicative 2nd singular would be 'dimittis' — 'dimitte' is imperative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive 2nd singular would be 'dimittas' — 'dimitte' is imperative

hints.mitto, mittere, misi, missum: These are the parts of 'mitto' (to send) — 'dimittere' is the compound dis- + mittere
hints.deminuo, deminuere, deminui, deminutum: These are the parts of 'deminuo' (to diminish)
hints.demo, demere, dempsi, demptum: These are the parts of 'demo' (to take away)

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sciant' — 'sciunt' is indicative

hints.sequor, sequi, secutus sum, —: These are the parts of 'sequor' (to follow) — a deponent verb
hints.scribo, scribere, scripsi, scriptum: These are the parts of 'scribo' (to write)
hints.sentio, sentire, sensi, sensum: These are the parts of 'sentio' (to feel/perceive)

steps.reveal: faciunt = they do / they are doing (3rd pl. pres.)

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'faciant' — 'faciunt' is indicative

hints.fio, fieri, factus sum, —: These are the parts of 'fio' (to become) — the passive of 'facio'
hints.fugio, fugere, fugi, fugitum: These are the parts of 'fugio' (to flee)
hints.fallo, fallere, fefelli, falsum: These are the parts of 'fallo' (to deceive)

hints.nominative: Nominative 'Pater' looks the same, but here it is direct address (vocative = nominative in 3rd decl.)
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'Patris' — 'Pater' is vocative here

steps.reveal: illis = them (dat. pl., indirect object of 'dimitte')

hints.ablative: Ablative 'illis' looks the same, but 'dimittere' + dative = forgive someone
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'illorum/illarum' — 'illis' is dative here

steps.prompt: What type of clause is 'quid faciunt'?
steps.reveal: non sciunt quid faciunt = they do not know what they are doing

hints.relative clause: A relative clause uses 'qui/quae/quod' — 'quid' is an interrogative introducing an indirect question
hints.result clause: Result clauses use 'ut' + subjunctive — 'quid' introduces an indirect question
hints.purpose clause: Purpose clauses use 'ut' + subjunctive — 'quid faciunt' is an indirect question

steps.prompt: Why is 'faciunt' indicative in the indirect question (instead of subjunctive)?
steps.target: Vulgate Latin often retains indicative in indirect questions (Biblical Latin usage)
choices: Vulgate Latin often retains indicative in indirect questions (Biblical Latin usage)
steps.reveal: Classical Latin would require subjunctive 'faciant', but the Vulgate uses indicative

hints.it is actually subjunctive: 'Faciunt' is present indicative — subjunctive would be 'faciant'
hints.indirect questions always take indicativ: In classical Latin, indirect questions require the subjunctive — the Vulgate is looser
hints.it is a result clause: This is an indirect question introduced by 'quid', not a result clause

translation: Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do
